title: Getting Started with GOcontext
text: GOcontext provides tools constructing restricted Gene Ontology (GO) graphs prior enrichment analysis. standard GO enrichment workflows, GO terms within ontology (Biological Process, Molecular Function, Cellular Component) tested simultaneously. However, many biological questions, large parts ontology irrelevant experimental system study. Testing terms unnecessarily increases number hypotheses therefore multiple testing burden. GOcontext addresses enabling users construct context-specific GO subgraphs performing enrichment analysis. package: loads GO structure GO.db attaches organism-specific GO annotations OrgDb restricts ontology graph mapped terms selected branches exports resulting mappings enrichment tools reducing number tested GO terms preserving underlying directed acyclic graph (DAG) structure, enrichment analyses can focus biologically relevant parts ontology maintaining transparent reproducible GO universe. typical workflow : load_go() → attach_org() → filter_mapped() → subset_go() → enrichment

title: Getting Started with GOcontext
Getting Started with GOcontext
text: GOcontext loads GO directly Bioconductor package GO.db. single ontology (BP, MF, CC) loaded time. returned object contains: GO term metadata parent–child edges defining GO DAG adjacency lists graph traversal GO.db version used construct graph
code: go_bp <- GOcontext::load_go("BP") ## ## 'select()' returned 1:1 mapping between keys and columns

title: Getting Started with GOcontext
text: GO enrichment requires mappings GO terms genes. obtained organism annotation package (OrgDb). attaches GO--gene mapping table GO graph object.
code: go_bp <- GOcontext::attach_org(     go = go_bp,     OrgDb = org.EcK12.eg.db ) ## 'select()' returned 1:many mapping between keys and columns

what: 3. Restrict the graph to mapped terms
title: Getting Started with GOcontext
text: Many GO terms gene annotations given organism. terms contribute enrichment results can therefore removed analysis. returned object GOSubgraph containing terms least one gene mapping. step often reduces ontology size substantially.

what: 4. Restrict the graph to selected branches
title: Getting Started with GOcontext
text: GOcontext allows restricting ontology graph specific regions. Two modes supported: keep – retain selected terms descendants exclude – remove selected terms descendants Example: restrict graph descendants particular GO term. returned GOSubgraph represents restricted GO graph. object contains: retained GO terms removed GO terms induced parent–child edges updated adjacency lists attached GO--gene mapping
code: go_sub <- GOcontext::subset_go(     go = go_bp_mapped,     ids = "GO:0006740",     mode = "keep" )

title: Getting Started with GOcontext
text: restricting graph, GO annotations can exported formats commonly used enrichment tools.

title: Getting Started with GOcontext
Getting Started with GOcontext
text: tables can supplied directly clusterProfiler::enricher().
code: term2name <- GOcontext::as_term2name(go_sub) head(term2name) ##         term                                          name ## 1 GO:0006098                       pentose-phosphate shunt ## 2 GO:0006740                            NADPH regeneration ## 3 GO:0009051     pentose-phosphate shunt, oxidative branch ## 4 GO:0009052 pentose-phosphate shunt, non-oxidative branch

title: Getting Started with GOcontext
text: Example workflow clusterProfiler: GO graph restricted beforehand, enrichment performed smaller biologically focused hypothesis space.
code: library(clusterProfiler)  enrichment_results <- clusterProfiler::enricher(     gene      = gene_list,     TERM2GENE = term2gene,     TERM2NAME = term2name )

title: Getting Started with GOcontext
text: GOcontext enables reproducible construction restricted GO graphs prior enrichment analysis. typical workflow consists : Loading GO using load_go() Attaching organism annotations attach_org() Restricting ontology mapped terms using filter_mapped() Restricting graph structure subset_go() Exporting GO mappings as_term2gene() as_term2name() Running enrichment analysis explicitly defining tested GO universe enrichment, GOcontext reduces multiple testing burden preserving ontology structure ensuring transparent analysis workflows.

title: Motivation
text: Gene Ontology (GO) one widely used resources functional enrichment analysis. Across three ontologies — Biological Process (BP), Molecular Function (MF), Cellular Component (CC) — thousands GO terms available potential hypotheses enrichment testing. many workflows, full ontology tested default. However, GO terms meaningful given organism biological question. result, enrichment analyses often include large numbers hypotheses biologically irrelevant. example, consider RNA-seq experiment performed E. coli. GO Biological Process ontology contains terms heart development, ossification, pregnancy, neuron differentiation. processes biologically impossible bacteria, yet remain part hypothesis space full ontology tested. practice, analysts often deal implicitly. Common strategies include filtering annotations, removing specific branches GO graph, restricting analyses annotated terms. However, steps frequently implemented small ad hoc scripts may explicitly documented. GOcontext introduce fundamentally new statistical method. Instead, provides tools make common practice — restricting GO hypothesis space prior enrichment analysis — easier transparent. Importantly, removing hypotheses priori invalidate statistical error control. Procedures Bonferroni (FWER control) Benjamini–Hochberg (FDR control) define guarantees respect set hypotheses actually tested. biologically irrelevant hypotheses excluded testing, statistical guarantees remain valid. time, reducing hypothesis space decreases multiple testing burden can therefore increase statistical power. following sections illustrate effect analytically simulation.

what: Analytical effect of hypothesis space reduction
title: Motivation
text: effect hypothesis restriction follows directly formulas multiple-testing correction procedures. Bonferroni correction (controlling family-wise error rate), rejection threshold p≤αm, p \le \frac{\alpha}{m}, mm denotes number tested hypotheses. hypothesis space reduced priori m′=(1−r)m m' = (1 - r)m rr proportion excluded hypotheses, new threshold becomes p≤αm′=α(1−r)m. p \le \frac{\alpha}{m'} = \frac{\alpha}{(1-r)m}. ratio new original thresholds therefore α/m′α/m=mm′=11−r. \frac{\alpha/m'}{\alpha/m} = \frac{m}{m'} = \frac{1}{1-r}. Thus, excluding 20% hypotheses (r=0.2r = 0.2) increases critical value factor 1/(1−0.2)=1.251/(1-0.2) = 1.25, corresponding 25% relaxation. scaling effect appears Benjamini–Hochberg (BH) false discovery rate procedure. rank kk, BH rejection rule p(k)≤kmq. p_{(k)} \le \frac{k}{m} q. restricting hypothesis space, p(k)≤km′q=k(1−r)mq. p_{(k)} \le \frac{k}{m'} q = \frac{k}{(1-r)m} q. Benjamini–Yekutieli () procedure introduces additional harmonic correction factor, dominant dependence 1/m1/m remains. cases, reducing number hypotheses produces deterministic relaxation rejection threshold.

title: Motivation
text: deterministic effect hypothesis restriction can summarized critical value multiplier, defined multiplier=mm′=11−r. \text{multiplier} = \frac{m}{m'} = \frac{1}{1-r}. multiplier depends excluded proportion rr independent observed data.  Figure 1. Critical value multiplier priori hypothesis restriction. multiplier represents factor statistical rejection threshold increases proportion rr hypotheses removed testing. excluded proportion increases, rejection boundary expands nonlinearly. Even moderate reductions hypothesis space can lead noticeable relaxation statistical thresholds.

what: Empirical power gains under Monte Carlo simulation
title: Motivation
text: examine deterministic relaxation critical values translates practical performance, performed Monte Carlo simulation study. Simulations conducted separately three GO ontologies (BP, MF, CC), using hypothesis counts approximating current sizes. ontology, number true signals fixed s=100. s = 100. Test statistics generated two-component mixture model: true signals sampled normal distribution mean shift (μ=3\mu = 3) null hypotheses sampled standard normal distribution Two-sided p-values computed resulting z-statistics. simulate priori hypothesis restriction, proportion rr null hypotheses removed testing, number true signals remained constant. reduced hypothesis space m′=(1−r)m m' = (1-r)m statistical power (true positive rate) estimated across repeated Monte Carlo replicates three multiple testing procedures: Bonferroni (FWER control) Benjamini–Hochberg (BH; FDR control) Benjamini–Yekutieli (; FDR control arbitrary dependence) Importantly, statistical procedure unchanged. number tested hypotheses reduced applying correction. Thus, observed gain power arises exclusively reduction multiplicity.  Figure 2. Empirical power gains priori hypothesis restriction Monte Carlo simulation. number tested hypotheses reduced; testing procedure remains unchanged. Across procedures ontology sizes, decreasing hypothesis space leads systematic increases statistical power. magnitude gain depends correction method total number tests follows deterministic scaling behavior derived .

title: Motivation
text: results illustrate simple principle: reducing number tested hypotheses can increase sensitivity without violating formal error control, provided restriction defined testing. context Gene Ontology analysis, many restrictions obvious biologically justified. Common examples include: removing GO terms without annotations organism interest excluding ontology branches irrelevant system study focusing analyses specific biological regions GO graph restrictions frequently applied informally enrichment analyses. Making explicit improves transparency reproducibility also reducing multiple testing burden. GOcontext provides simple tools construct restricted GO graphs export resulting annotation mappings enrichment workflows. ```r sessionInfo()

what: Context-Aware Restriction of the Gene Ontology for Enrichment Analysis
title: Context-Aware Restriction of the Gene Ontology for Enrichment Analysis
text: Context-aware restriction Gene Ontology (GO) hypothesis space enrichment analysis. GOcontext constructs context-specific GO subgraphs restrict set GO terms tested enrichment analysis. Starting Gene Ontology graph distributed GO.db, organism-specific GO--gene mappings can attached OrgDb, ontology can restricted terms relevant current biological context. reducing number tested GO terms preserving underlying GO graph structure, GOcontext aims decrease multiple testing burden maintaining transparent reproducible GO universe enrichment analysis. See full documentation GOcontext website.

title: Context-Aware Restriction of the Gene Ontology for Enrichment Analysis
code: if (!requireNamespace("remotes", quietly = TRUE)) {   install.packages("remotes") }  remotes::install_github(   "Thomas-Rauter/GOcontext",   # GitHub repository   ref = "<tag>",        # Version to install, e.g. v0.1.0    dependencies = TRUE,  # Install all dependencies   upgrade = "always"    # Always upgrade dependencies   # force = TRUE        # when encountering issues )

title: Context-Aware Restriction of the Gene Ontology for Enrichment Analysis
text: minimal, conceptual workflow:
code: library(GOcontext) library(org.EcK12.eg.db)  # 1) Load the GO DAG for one ontology go <- load_go("BP")  # 2) Attach organism annotation go <- attach_org(     go    = go,     OrgDb = org.EcK12.eg.db )  # 3) Restrict the graph to GO terms with mapped genes go <- filter_mapped(go)  # 4) Interactively browse the GO hierarchy sel <- browse_go_tree(go)  # 5) Restrict the graph to the selected GO branches go_sub <- subset_go(     go   = go,     ids  = sel$ids,     mode = "keep" )  # 6) Export enrichment tables term2gene <- as_term2gene(go_sub) term2name <- as_term2name(go_sub)  # 7) Run enrichment analysis clusterProfiler::enricher(     gene      = genes_of_interest,     TERM2GENE = term2gene,     TERM2NAME = term2name,     universe  = background_genes )

what: Load a GO sub-ontology graph from GO.db — load_go
title: Load a GO sub-ontology graph from GO.db — load_go
text: Loads single Gene Ontology (GO) sub-ontology (BP, MF, CC) Bioconductor annotation package GO.db constructs -memory directed acyclic graph (DAG) representation. returned GO object contains GO term metadata together parent–child relationships required graph traversal downstream restriction operations. ontology snapshot corresponds installed version GO.db, recorded returned object reproducibility.

title: Load a GO sub-ontology graph from GO.db — load_go
Load a GO sub-ontology graph from GO.db — load_go
text: ont character(1) GO sub-ontology load. One "BP", "MF", "CC". include_obsolete logical(1) Whether obsolete GO terms included. FALSE (default), obsolete terms removed graph.

title: Load a GO sub-ontology graph from GO.db — load_go
text: GO S4 object representing requested GO sub-ontology. object contains: GO term metadata parent–child relationships defining ontology DAG adjacency lists efficient graph traversal empty GO--gene mapping table populated         attach_org() GO.db version used construct graph

title: Load a GO sub-ontology graph from GO.db — load_go
text: ontology graph loaded load_go() serves starting point GO restriction workflows implemented package. Organism-specific mappings can attached using attach_org(), graph can subsequently restricted using functions filter_mapped() subset_go().
